# Questions in Livestream 209

Live Stream #209: One-Meaning Flippancy

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=520" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:40</a> | If the exodus happened under Ramesses II, why did he remain highly esteemed instead of being remembered as a pharaoh who failed to preserve *ma'at*? | He argues the exodus would have happened only about halfway through Ramesses II's long reign, leaving decades for image repair through later monuments, temples, and royal memory-shaping. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=738" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:18</a> | Were the Egyptian gods originally based on humans who were later deified? | Generally no; he says the gods do not appear to be former humans, apart from a few later back-attributions tied to tomb traditions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=832" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:52</a> | Weren't all pharaohs gods in a sense? | Not in a simple sense; he distinguishes Egyptian apotheosis and royal/divine status from saying every pharaoh was originally a god. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=1165" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:25</a> | How should the famous "helicopter," "jet," and "light bulb" carvings be explained? | He says the "helicopter" and "jet" are palimpsests, with one inscription carved over another, and the "light bulb" is a stylized lotus, not evidence of advanced ancient technology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=1358" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:38</a> | Why is Abraham called God's friend? | He says Abraham's heart was aligned toward doing God's will and serving God willingly, even though Abraham still sinned like everyone else. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=1501" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:01</a> | Would Abraham have worn Canaanite clothing or clothing from his ancestral homeland? | More Canaanite-style pastoral clothing, because his life as a sojourning shepherd would differ from the city life of his Mesopotamian homeland. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=1585" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:25</a> | Are cognate studies still a safe path for someone who wants to become an Old Testament scholar? | He says the hiring trend has shifted away from it, but he still strongly recommends cognate study because the training quality is often excellent. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=1937" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:17</a> | Would Moses have known he was a Hebrew? | Probably not in the later ethnic sense; he would have known he was a son of Jacob, but the Hebrew identity as an ethnonym had not yet formed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=2063" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:23</a> | Are all Hebrews bums, but not all bums Hebrews? | No. He distinguishes *hapiru* as a class term for outcasts or sojourners from Hebrew as a later language or ethnic designation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=2260" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:40</a> | What was the pre-exodus religious practice at Avaris? | He says Avaris was dominated by Canaanite worship, especially Baal, along with deities such as Astarte and Resheph. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=2473" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:13</a> | What ancient Near Eastern peoples had seven-day weeks? | He says the clearest early example he knows is Israel; Egypt had ten-day weeks, Rome had an eight-day cycle, and Mesopotamian or Canaanite evidence is uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=2587" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:07</a> | Were the people in Luke 8 Gadites rather than Greeks? | Yes. He says the region belonged to Gad, with Reuben and Manasseh nearby, so Jesus was speaking to Israelites there. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=2755" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:55</a> | What was the living situation of the Ramesside family during and after Moses's birth? | He places the Ramessides in the delta, likely around Avaris, with their position changing as dynastic politics and Moses's adoption developed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=2892" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:12</a> | Was Moses ever in line for the Egyptian throne? | No. He says succession required royal birth through a queen or dynastic crisis, and Moses did not qualify. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=3039" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:39</a> | Do we have any clue which son of Ramesses may have died in the plague? | He says the likeliest candidate is Amun-her-khepeshef, Ramesses II's firstborn crown prince, who disappears around the twenty-fifth regnal year. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=3524" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:44</a> | What biblical people or places would later forgers not know about but have been verified? | He gave Pi-hahiroth as an obscure Exodus place verified in Egyptian texts and unlikely to be known by later writers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=4320" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:00</a> | Did the Philistines reach Mount Gilboa by penetrating the highlands or through the Jezreel Valley? | He said they entered by the Jezreel Valley, a common and easier route for armies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=4423" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:43</a> | Besides Thoth, were there Egyptian gods that came from body parts or fluids of other gods? | Yes. He said some gods, especially minor ones, could arise from body parts, procreation, or self-creation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=4576" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:16</a> | Since you have mentioned being in Canada for the glory of God, what are you doing up there? | He answers personally and jokingly that he lives in Vancouver, finds it expensive and frustrating, and would return to the United States if he could. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=5144" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:44</a> | How would you respond to David Bentley Hart's claim that Genesis must be spiritualized or becomes myth? | He called that a false dichotomy, since other readings exist and Genesis does not say the garden trees were for gods to eat. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=5310" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:28:30</a> | Would you suggest that myth is a kind of language? | He says myth is not quite a language, but it is a kind of expression that can communicate meaning while still referring to something understood as literal. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=5499" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:39</a> | In Genesis 6:1-5, was it a sin for angels to marry human women? | He said he does not think they were angels; "sons of God" can mean other roles, and angels do not marry or procreate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=5795" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:35</a> | What does it mean to "come down" in the Genesis 6 passage? | He says the text does not say they came down; it says they came in, which he reads as a euphemism for sex. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=5855" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:35</a> | Could Genesis 6:3 be translated as God's Spirit striving "in" man forever? | He says "with" is the clearer reading, while stretching it to "in" would not be his choice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=5918" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:38</a> | Does the text explicitly identify the Nephilim as offspring of the sons of God and daughters of men? | He says yes; the clause implies that the Nephilim resulted from that union. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=6082" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:22</a> | Why would the Nephilim's attributes result directly from this union, and why would they be men of renown? | He argues that breaking a religious vow was thought to produce socially disruptive figures, so "men of renown" is negative in context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=6170" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:50</a> | Is "renown" not a good thing in this context? | Correct; he says "men of renown" is not a compliment and "men of infamy" would better convey the idea. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=6525" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:45</a> | Is Genesis 6 an example of the sins of the fathers being passed down? | Yes. He says it is one of the original examples: bad priests produce bad sons, and the children become worse than the fathers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=6668" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:08</a> | How should Genesis 5:21-24 be interpreted with the clan theory in mind? | He reads Enoch as a clan-ending progenitor whose extraordinary taking by God causes the whole clan history to be credited to him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=7003" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:43</a> | In Enoch's day, was his clan not yet named for him? | He says probably not; the earlier name is unknown, and Enoch may be the later figure after whom the clan is remembered. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=7120" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:40</a> | Does Genesis 5:24 mean God literally took Enoch to heaven instead of Enoch dying a normal physical death? | Yes; he reads the verse as saying Enoch was taken and did not undergo an ordinary death. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=7340" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:20</a> | Does "God Most High, creator of heaven and earth" in Genesis 14:19-22 appear in Ugaritic texts? | He is not sure without checking, but says *El Elyon* does not appear in Ugaritic texts while *El* does; Ugaritic evidence is also several centuries later than Abraham. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=7529" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:29</a> | If God's energy can punish wrongdoers in hell, why did God not do that when he created the world? | He says immediate summary judgment would have ended all humanity without grace or mercy, while delayed judgment allows God's mercy and patience to be shown. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=7846" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:46</a> | Does Revelation 1:18 mean Jesus is the warden of hell? | No. He says the keys of death and Hades mean Christ has conquered death and controls release from the realm of the dead, so he is a liberator rather than a warden. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=8044" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:04</a> | If Revelation 1:18 meant hell, would it have said Gehenna? | Not necessarily; he says Gehenna refers to the lake of fire, while Hades is the realm of the dead, and "hell" is a broad later term. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=8334" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:54</a> | Did Egyptian gods get new characteristics or behaviors after a pharaoh, and were new gods constantly being created? | Generally no; he says traits were not borrowed from kings, though cults could borrow traits from other religious movements, and gods were more often added than created. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=8850" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:30</a> | How much would it cost to pay you for an academic book review? | He says he is very slow with book reviews and is still working through a promised review, so the answer is effectively that it would take time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=8952" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:12</a> | Out of LDS, fundamentalist, and liberal scholarly circles, which is the worst? | He says it is hard to answer because each has different problems: LDS is outside historic Christianity, fundamentalism is hyperliteral and parochial, and liberal scholarship is not Christian. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=8998" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:58</a> | How is the video game development coming? | He says it is going well, with five of seven endings finished and more asset work still to do. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=9058" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:58</a> | Was Joseph Smith's Egyptian grammar on par with the understanding level of his time? | He says it was far below par because Champollion had already deciphered Egyptian, though that knowledge had not reached Joseph Smith's level. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=9264" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:24</a> | Why do you oppose Christian nationalism conceptually, and how does it differ from medieval systems? | He rejects it as a movement combining legalism and racism, saying it would impose state control on church life and contradict Christian liberty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=9390" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:30</a> | What denominations would you consider to be Christian nationalist? | He says he does not know of denominations that are Christian nationalist; he describes the movement as driven by individual platformed voices. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=9518" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:38:38</a> | Will Christian nationalists be among those who hear "Depart from me, I never knew you"? | He answers yes, then jokes with the wording; the serious direction is that he views the movement as spiritually dangerous. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=9745" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:25</a> | Do the Philistine-inhabited lands roughly correspond to today's Gaza Strip? | No. He says Philistine territory in the Iron Age was much more expansive, extending up the coastal plain and into the western Shephelah. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=9816" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:36</a> | What extra-biblical information do we have on Abraham's father Terah? | He points to five Mari letters that he says fit Terah's name, time, place, son dying, political turmoil, and sojourner status. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=9926" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:26</a> | Are there counterfactuals or detail mismatches in the Mari evidence for Terah? | He says the main mismatch is Ur/Kesh of the Chaldees, which may be an anachronism, misspelling, or spacing issue that can be resolved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10009" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:49</a> | Do the Mari letters mention Terah by name? | Yes. He says the letters use a form like Teru, which he treats as Terah with the expected terminal consonant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10153" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:13</a> | Do you think the Jasher tablet is fake? | He thinks it probably is, but says he would need to look closely; he distinguishes that tablet from other evidence for Jasher. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10192" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:52</a> | What is the difference between early biblical history and biblical prehistory? | He says history is defined by written records or external sources, so figures attested in letters move from prehistory into history. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10270" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:10</a> | What are your thoughts on Benjamin Sommer's claim that God has multiple fluid and sometimes simultaneous bodies? | He has not read the work closely enough to comment, but urges caution because that conclusion can get weird quickly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10330" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:10</a> | Would oral history be considered prehistory? | Often, but not always; he says oral history can overlap with recorded history and is not mutually exclusive with it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10401" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:21</a> | Did Jewish priests take a vow of silence, as with Zacharias in Luke? | He says Torah and later texts do not describe such a priestly vow, so Zacharias's silence appears unique or unexplained. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10478" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:38</a> | Is Canaan the product of Ham and Noah's wife, explaining Noah's curse, and is this a clan-usurpation attempt? | He says Canaan is the product of that act and that explains the curse; clan usurpation is possible, but not certain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10830" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:30</a> | What about Shem and Japheth walking backward; would just looking be considered a sex act? | He says looking would still be an act of lewdness toward the naked woman involved, and the backward walking indicates they were covering a woman. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10891" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:31</a> | Would it be sinful to rewrite the Bible into modern language and remove the euphemisms? | No. He says removing euphemisms and updating contextual readings could be good, though controversial. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=11230" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:07:10</a> | Who do you think the historical Amalekites were? | Tentatively, he identifies them with the Lukka, a Sea Peoples group, but says the argument still needs to be written and published. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=11384" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:44</a> | If the Amalekites were Indo-European, did they have a language related to modern English? | Only distantly; he says English is Germanic, while the Amalekites would more likely be closer to Hittite or another Anatolian Indo-European branch. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=11520" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:00</a> | Will your newly published chronology paper be available on Sci-Hub? | No; he says it will be open source somewhere, but he still needs details, and he will also post a copy on Patreon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=11641" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:14:01</a> | Do you plan to share this chronology research at the next Evangelical Theological Society conference? | No, because he says it is a paper on chronology and chronology methods, not theology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=11793" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:33</a> | Is Archaeometry a famous academic journal? | Yes. He identifies the journal as *Archaeometry* and treats it as a significant academic venue. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=12028" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:28</a> | Why don't researchers expose peer reviewers who are insulting like that? | He says they sometimes do, especially when reviewers reveal their names despite nominal anonymity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=12093" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:33</a> | Did the Hittites' use of "king of Ahhiyawa" suggest a lack of understanding of Greek political culture, and are there parallels elsewhere? | Yes. He says cross-cultural diplomatic misunderstandings happened among Hittites, Egyptians, Assyrians, Mitanni, Greeks, and others. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=12344" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:44</a> | Is the Ipuwer Papyrus an accurate depiction of the Exodus? | No. He says it describes earlier plague-like hypernatural events, not the Exodus itself, which he places centuries later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=12500" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:20</a> | Does Heiser's holy stapler analogy discredit the prophetic nature of Isaiah 53? | He passes because he does not know that analogy well enough to comment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=12536" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:56</a> | Could you explain Leviticus 19:2 in context with the rest of chapter 19? | He says holiness in Leviticus 19 means keeping the law, doing justice, obeying applicable ritual law, and maintaining righteousness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=12883" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34:43</a> | How can someone in third-year Greek learn to care about old languages? | He advises treating study as worship and remembering that the usefulness of Greek often becomes clear after seminary through later reading and ministry. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=13154" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:14</a> | Should Greek still be confusing in third year? | Yes. He says continued confusion means the student is seeing the real difficulties of advanced Greek, especially with genitives and participles. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=13232" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:32</a> | Is Hebrew easier than Greek? | He says Hebrew is harder for the first couple of months because of the script and Masoretic points, but the grammar gets easier over time, while Greek gets harder. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=13346" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:42:26</a> | If someone plans to teach Old Testament at seminary, will Hebrew be heavily required and will Greek still matter? | Yes. He says Hebrew is essential, comparative languages may matter, and Greek still matters for Septuagint and textual criticism work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=13821" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:21</a> | What is the biggest "I told you so" moment in biblical archaeology? | He points to the Tel Dan Stele, especially its reference to the house of David against earlier minimalist denials of a Davidic kingdom. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=13936" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:16</a> | Do you think scholars will ever have a similar "I told you so" moment with Moses? | It is possible, but he is not optimistic about a direct smoking-gun Moses inscription because of how the exodus worked. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=14104" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:04</a> | Have you seen the Joe Rogan interview with Zahi Hawass? | No. He says he has not seen it and avoids saying much about Hawass because Hawass still has power in Egypt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=14183" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:56:23</a> | Is there a book recommendation on different views of the supplementary hypothesis compared with the documentary hypothesis? | He does not know of a good book yet, but recommends the writings of Benjamin Kilchör, whom he identifies as a supplementary-hypothesis proponent and documentary-hypothesis critic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=14231" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:57:11</a> | Is Zahi Hawass Muslim? | Yes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=14248" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:57:28</a> | Does Jonah 2:2-7 show that Jonah died inside the fish, especially since similar language appears in Psalm 30? | He says Jonah 2 does present Jonah as descending to death, while Psalm 30 uses similar imagery differently and should not be flattened into the same meaning. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=14959" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:09:19</a> | Does the people determinative with "Israel" on the Merneptah Stele show that Merneptah encountered Israel away from its home territory? | No; he says the people determinative alone does not prove that, but the throwing-stick determinative and the poetic foreign-nations context do support Israel being treated as a foreign people group. |
